skillName: Angles around a point sum to 360°
misconceptionSummary: Students confuse angles around a point (360°) with angles on a straight line (180°) and subtract from 180° instead of 360°.

lines.text: Angles around a point: 100°, 50°, and y°
Used 180° — but these angles go all the way round

steps.narration: The student used one hundred and eighty instead of three hundred and sixty. But these three angles go all the way around the point in every direction — that is a full turn, not a straight line. One hundred and eighty applies only when angles are on one side of a straight line.

visuals.ruleText: Ask: do these angles go all the way around, or only on one side of a line?
visuals.subText: All the way around → 360°.   One side of a straight line → 180°.

steps.narration: Before writing the equation, ask: do these angles surround the point completely, or are they on just one side of a straight line? Completely surrounding means three hundred and sixty. One side of a line means one hundred and eighty.

lines.text: Angles all the way around: 100° + 50° + y° = 360°

steps.narration: With three hundred and sixty as the total: one hundred plus fifty plus y equals three hundred and sixty. Y equals two hundred and ten — a reflex angle. Check: one hundred plus fifty plus two hundred and ten is three hundred and sixty. Correct.

Two hundred and ten degrees — a reflex angle completing the full rotation. Three hundred and sixty for a full point, one hundred and eighty for a straight line. The geometry of the situation determines which to use.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: 180° − 100° − 50° = 30° for angles around a full point. Full rotation around a point is 360°, not 180°. The answer is 360° − 150° = 210°.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Straight line gives 180°; full point gives 360°. If the angles go all the way around, use 360°. If they are on just one side of a line, use 180°. The diagram tells you which situation applies.
